did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Artificial Intelligence, specifically FastCUT (an unsupervised computer image-to-image translation model) and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using the Inception-ResNet-V2 architecture via Keras, to classify Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and melanoma in dermatology. FastCUT was used to artificially darken skin tones in images to improve the CNN's performance on darker-skinned individuals.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the use of FastCUT (unsupervised computer image-to-image translation) and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) using Inception-ResNet-V2 architecture to classify Basal Cell Carcinoma and melanoma. It includes specific Methods, Results (including sensitivity, specificity, F1 scores, and AUC values in Table 1), and a Conclusion based on new data generated from 553 BCC and 324 melanoma images. The abstract explicitly states the objective was to improve AI model performance through a specific intervention, which is characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Artificial intelligence image recognition of melanoma and basal cell carcinoma in racially diverse populations
brief_summary: This study aimed to improve the performance of artificial intelligence models in diagnosing basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and melanoma in individuals with darker skin tones, where model accuracy typically suffers due to data paucity. The researchers utilized FastCUT, an unsupervised image-to-image translation model, to artificially darken the skin tones of 553 BCC and 324 melanoma images sourced from various dermatological databases while preserving lesion characteristics. These modified images were used to train a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on the Inception-ResNet-V2 architecture via Keras. The model's performance was evaluated on a separate set of 'naïve' dark-skinned images using metrics including sensitivity, specificity, F1 score, and area under the receiver-operating characteristic curve (AUC). The results demonstrated that training on artificially darkened images significantly improved diagnostic accuracy compared to training on original light-skinned images, with AUC values increasing from 0.64 to 0.76 for BCC and from 0.65 to 0.81 for melanoma. The study concludes that unsupervised image translation is a viable method to enhance AI diagnostic accuracy for racially diverse skin tones.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as a primary diagnostic target. 553 images were used (261 darker-skinned, 292 light-skinned). The CNN model differentiated BCC from melanoma.
Studied as a primary diagnostic target. 324 images were used (102 darker-skinned, 222 light-skinned). The CNN model differentiated melanoma from BCC.

all_task_types.target: Differentiating between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and melanoma
all_task_types.details: The study uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on Inception-ResNet-V2 to classify skin lesions into discrete diagnostic categories (BCC vs. melanoma). Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, F1 score, and Area Under the Receiver-Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC). The model predicts binary class labels for each image.

datasets_used.description: A public dermatological database used to acquire real and unstandardized skin lesion images.

use.validation: Source of original light-skinned BCC and melanoma images used for training and validation.
use.other: Source of images for FastCUT unsupervised image-to-image translation (darkening).

datasets_used.description: A public dermatological database used to acquire real and unstandardized skin lesion images.

use.validation: Source of original light-skinned BCC and melanoma images used for training and validation.
use.other: Source of images for FastCUT unsupervised image-to-image translation (darkening).

datasets_used.description: A public dermatological database used to acquire real and unstandardized skin lesion images.

datasets_used.name: Combined Dermatology Database (Light-Skinned)
datasets_used.description: The aggregate dataset of 553 BCC and 324 melanoma images sourced from the four public databases listed above, specifically the light-skinned subset used for model development.

use.training: 80% of these images (light-skinned BCC and melanoma) were used to train the CNN.
use.validation: 20% of these images (light-skinned BCC and melanoma) were used to validate the CNN.
use.other: Used as input for FastCUT to generate artificially darkened images.

datasets_used.name: Naïve Dark-Skinned Test Set
datasets_used.description: A separate set of 'naïve' dark-skinned images that were not provided to FastCUT for darkening, used to evaluate the model's performance on darker skin tones.

use.testing: Used to test the performance of the CNN models trained on original light-skinned images and artificially darkened images.

statistical_methods_used.method: Sensitivity, Specificity, Positive Predictive Value (PPV), Negative Predictive Value (NPV), F1 score
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate and compare the diagnostic performance of the CNN models on 'naïve' dark-skinned test images.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver-operating characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess and compare the classification performance of the CNN models for BCC and melanoma.
To perform statistical analysis of the CNN output, including calculation of metrics and creation of ROC curves.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance improvements were described using comparative metrics (sensitivity, specificity, F1 score, AUC) without statistical significance testing.

Naïve Dark-Skinned Test Set.description: Performance of CNN models (Inception-ResNet-V2) trained on original light-skinned images vs. artificially 'darkened' light-skinned images, tested on 303 naïve dark-skinned images (231 BCC, 72 Melanoma).
Naïve Dark-Skinned Test Set.notes: Training on artificially darkened images resulted in improved sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, F1 score, and AUC for both BCC and melanoma compared to training on original light-skinned images when tested on dark-skinned individuals.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on Inception-ResNet-V2 architecture
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained with the ImageNet dataset (containing more than 14 million images)

architecture_details.notes: No pre-processing (cropping, translation, brightness, etc.) was performed on the input images to limit variability.

training_details.training_schedule: Error stop was implemented to stop training when validation loss was at a minimum.
training_details.data_split: 80% of light-skinned images used for training, 20% for validation.

preprocessing_techniques: No pre-processing (cropping, translation, brightness, etc.) was performed on the input images to limit variability.
No pre-processing or altering of the images was performed to reduce bias in model development.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Fast Contrastive Unpaired Translation (FastCUT) was used to artificially darken skin tones in images of light-skinned individuals (Fitzpatrick skin types 1-2) to simulate darker skin tones (Fitzpatrick skin types 4-5) while preserving dermatological disease/lesion characteristics. This synthetic data generation technique was applied to 292 light-skinned BCC images and 222 light-skinned melanoma images. The model was trained using only 30 dark-skinned images as reference for the translation.

conclusions: Training an AI model on artificially 'darkened' images resulted in higher sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, F1 score, and area under the receiver-operating characteristic curve (AUC) for differentiating between basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and melanoma in individuals with brown skin tone compared to training on original 'light-skinned' images.
The AUC for BCC classification improved from 0.64 to 0.76, and for melanoma classification improved from 0.65 to 0.81 when using the model trained on darkened images.

Use of unsupervised image-to-image translation in medical AI image recognition models has the potential to significantly improve their accuracy in diagnosing diseases in individuals with racially diverse skin tone.

Unsupervised computer darkening of skin color preserves dermatological disease/lesion characteristics while allowing for the enhancement of AI model performance on darker skin tones without requiring a large number of real dark-skinned images for training.
important_remarks: The study relies on unsupervised image-to-image translation (FastCUT) to artificially darken skin tones, which is a synthetic data augmentation method rather than using real dark-skinned images for training.
The FastCUT model was trained using only 30 dark-skinned reference images for BCC and 30 for melanoma, which is a very small sample size for the translation model.
The 'naïve' test set consisted of only 303 images (231 BCC, 72 Melanoma), which is a small sample size for evaluating clinical performance.
The study did not perform formal hypothesis testing; no p-values or statistical significance tests were reported for the performance improvements.

The dataset is derived from public dermatological databases (Hellenic Dermatological Atlas, Dermatology Atlas, Interactive Dermatology Atlas, DermNet NZ), which may introduce selection bias as these are not standardized clinical datasets.
The authors note that the 'darkened' images are not 'blackened' and that further darkening may degrade model performance, indicating a limitation in the range of skin tones simulated.
The study acknowledges that disease manifestations can vary between light and dark skin tones, and the translation method may not fully capture these clinical differences.
No external validation was performed; the model was tested on a separate 'naïve' set from the same source databases, not an independent external cohort.
The study did not use data augmentation techniques like cropping, translation, or rotation to limit variability, which may limit the generalizability of the CNN model.
The improvement in AUC for BCC was from 0.64 to 0.76 and for melanoma from 0.65 to 0.81, which, while statistically notable in the context of the study, still represents moderate diagnostic accuracy.
